turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument this morning in Case 24-7351, Pitchford versus Cain. Mr. Perkovich.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court: In the selection of Terry Pitchford's capital jury in 2006, the trial court grasped and conducted just two of Batson's three steps after the district attorney struck in succession four black citizens. Despite the state's assertions throughout the red brief, the judge never determined the credibility of the prosecutor's step 2 proffers. Had the judge done what Batson demands in step 3, the court would have considered, from the voir dire that single February morning, the prosecutor's absence of questioning about the issues and the proffers' lack of record support and irrelevance to the case. What is more, proper credibility determinations would have concerned numerous extreme bad-faith findings against this district attorney in two Mississippi Supreme Court capital reversals published in 2003 and 2000, which held this prosecutor had fabricated prior statements to impeach four witnesses who were black and, in closing argument, espoused 14 discrete lies about the record. Instead of abiding Batson, Pitchford's trial court careened to opening arguments. When the defense strained to be heard from -- before the jury's empanelment both about Batson and a fair cross-section challenge, the judge assured her three times the Batson objections were preserved. Yet the Mississippi Supreme Court found that Pitchford's defense failed to rebut the proffers and thus waived argument, while deeming the merely race-neutral proffers as "acceptable," a pale substitute for Batson's demands and a sidestep of the trial court's failure to determine the credibility of the four strikes. The trial court's own rectified failings in this prosecution, also riddled with other misconduct, yielded a jury selected with discriminatory taint, which in turn condemned an 18-year-old whose accomplice, according to the state's case, killed the shopkeeper in this botched robbery. We urge this Court to reinforce the Equal Protection Clause's guarantees for defendants as well as citizens willing to accept the awesome responsibility of jury service. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Did trial counsel make those arguments?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Mr. -- Justice Thomas, which arguments specifically do you mean?

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Did -- did the trial counsel make the argument that the prosecution discriminatorily picked the -- the jury or exercised peremptory strikes --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Trial counsel --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: -- for the reasons you just stated?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, trial counsel made the objection timely and --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: What was the objection?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: It was a Batson objection.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Okay. And did -- did -- trial counsel has the burden of demonstrating discrimination, right?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, that's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: So did trial counsel do that?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, trial counsel attempted to engage that process, which entails three steps and burden-shifting. Unfortunately, the third step did not occur in this case, notwithstanding trial counsel's effort.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: What do you mean by that, it didn't occur? The judge decided that it was race-neutral.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Respectfully, Justice Thomas, yes, that's all the judge did.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: But did the trial counsel offer an argument or evidence that the reasons offered by the prosecutor were pretextual?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, as the -- the venire was being released from the court, trial counsel sought to be heard on Batson and a fair cross-section issue and -- and was told by the trial judge repeatedly that her record was preserved.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: But didn't trial counsel subsequently file an affidavit indicating that she did not raise these objections?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, yes, that's correct. Three years after the decision that's before the Court now in state post-conviction, an affidavit supporting a post- -- an ineffective assistance of post-conviction counsel -- or convict -- trial counsel was authored which hews very closely to the decisions before the Court. It just mirrors what was ruled by the state supreme court in 2010.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What the transcript -- transcript shows is that Ms. Steiner's -- what she said is: At some point, the defense is going to want to reserve both its Batson objection and straight Fourteenth Amendment racial discrimination. Was that objection raised later on? I mean, I think -- I think that's where -- that's the preface to where you say three times the court said you -- you have that. And I wonder if there was some confusion between the -- the reference to "At some point, the defense is going to want to reserve" and the court's statements that it is -- "you've already made it in the record, so I am of the opinion that it is in the record."

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, thank you for that question. It allows me to clarify the sort of -- the goalposts on this question under Mississippi procedure. When the venire is dismissed and -- and the jury's empaneled, the opportunity to challenge Batson essentially ends or does end. Of course, there are, you know, posttrial motion practice that can occur. So the relevant time for this to be addressed, which defense counsel was aware of, and that's why she strived to speak to that issue in a separate fair cross-section issue, when it was still relevant for the trial court's decision, so I don't -- I don't think that the -- the defense counsel was seeking to sort of put a marker to be able to return to it because there's really -- that was the point of no return for -- for the judge.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but she did say, at some point, she's going to want to reserve. I just wonder if that's a source of some confusion for the judge or what we're supposed to do with that potential.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, Mr. Chief Justice, I think, if you see sort of as the colloquy goes down the page with, as you pointed to, it's clear in the record three times; then the judge turns to reiterating his ruling, which, again, is merely a step 2 ruling as to race-neutral findings, and he says so there is no Batson violation, drawing a line under the Batson colloquy at that point and then shifting to the other issues, meaning that fair cross-section challenge I spoke of.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Could we interpret "at some" -- "at some point" from the defense counsel as an indication that she was preparing or prepared to make the showing and she was asking the court for the opportunity to do so?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Jackson, yes, I think that that is the fair reading and an unmistakable reading of this record.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I mean, it does -- it does indicate that she was suggesting that she would like to do something. It wasn't as though she felt as though the Batson objection was already -- or at least her arguments were perfected, and it sounds to me from the transcript that the trial court was cutting her off, was not giving her a chance --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: -- was maybe mistakenly saying that it had already been done.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Jackson. If I could pick up on that last point. I -- I think the cold record here, and it is a cold record we're working with, of course, indicates that if you look at page 168 in the JA in terms of that initial colloquy when the Batson challenge was raised and then Miller-El was invoked, the judge is -- is sort of reflecting a disorientation to what the Batson inquiry holds, which is three steps. He is speaking to the race-neutral proffers that, you know, correctly are the second step, but he's unclear whether this goes to all the stricken venire members or just the black stricken venire members on 168. And so you fast-forward to the proffers that are put forward by the prosecutor and you see in rapid fire within a page the finding race-neutral, race-neutral, race-neutral, race-neutral, let's return to striking the jury, and -- and the court instructs the defense to continue the peremptory strikes. And so, at the first moment where there is an opportunity to speak to this, defense counsel returns to the issue and seeks to be heard on it and, as noted, is told three times in a row it's clear in the record and then a reiteration of the ruling.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, after the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But, I guess --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- after the -- the point the Chief Justice raised, at some point, the -- the court responded you've already made it in the record, so I'm of the opinion it is in the record. And then the prosecutor -- I mean the defense counsel says, well, I don't want to let the paneling go by without having those objections. And then the court says I think you've already made those and they are clear in the record. For the reasons previously stated, the court finds the reasons were race-neutral, right? So it -- it continues on. I guess how we read that confusion is -- is critical to whether there's a waiver here or whether it was unreasonable to find a waiver here.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That's right, Justice Kavanaugh. And, of course, you know, that addresses the D2 considerations in the case, and there are legal implications as well in terms of the mishandling of this, but reading that record and determining whether this is a reasonable determination that this defense counsel on this transcript had forfeited or -- or failed to rebut when her effort to be heard on the matter was clearly addressed and -- and so her --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- in the posttrial motion that was made here, the jury was empaneled right after the exchange the Chief made, so there was --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- no time to make a record there.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: In the posttrial motion that was made by plaintiff's -- by Petitioner's counsel, I believe she did raise a Batson challenge and she did raise the pretext argument directly, didn't she?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor, that's right. And that's very salient because that is on this record the first opportunity meaningfully to speak to the pretext question in comparison to issue which he did --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Once the -- once the judge decided to empanel the jury, the only time really to raise it again was on posttrial motions?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And she did. She said the reasons were pretext given -- I -- I don't have the exact language or maybe you have it memorized -- but given the -- the same -- the voir dire was similar, they gave answers similar to the general public to the other people in the pool, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Sotomayor. And that's drawing from questionnaire responses because there was no voir dire to speak of of the stricken individuals here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Counsel, can I ask you how you think that the voir dire or the interchange, sorry, between counsel and the district -- and the judge should have gone? One thing I find challenging in reading the transcript, it doesn't record pauses, right, it doesn't record seconds, and so, if we had a transcript here where the -- the judge said, sit down, counsel, I don't want to hear anything more from you --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: -- that would be a lot clearer, right? I mean, but we're in D2, so, you know, the state's getting a lot of deference here. What do you think the judge should have done?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, what we see commonplace in our courts is the court turning to the defense and offering the floor in some fashion. Agreed, we don't have sort of an audio recording. We can't sense whether there's a pause here, but I think the context is really important. And this is a jury selection process with a voir dire that took about three hours. To put it in context, the Miller-El case, obviously, it's really important here --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But I -- but -- but you're not really answering my question. When she raised the objection, when she said she wanted to preserve it for the record, all she had done at this point -- I mean, you're right, there are three steps, but the -- so she raised the Batson objection. The prosecutor advanced the race-neutral reason. And she had the opportunity to impeach that reason essentially. Right?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Respectfully, no, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Well, so -- well, let's see. That would be the third step. What you're saying is that the judge cut her off before she reached that step, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: I mean, I -- I understood the fair cross-section point to be part of her effort to impeach, not just a distinct argument. Am I understanding that wrong?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, I'll point you to JA 161 through 163, where there is a submission of the fair cross-section argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So she wasn't making that point also in reference to the Batson challenge?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: There's some overlap because of --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Some overlap?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: -- of the general issue with the jury, but it's a discrete challenge.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Discrete challenge.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: And that shows up in the appellee's brief and the -- and the ultimate decision in the case as well.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. So I guess here's -- here's my specific question. If you could tell me what you think the judge should have done, because she raises the objection. The prosecutor offers a race-neutral reason.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: She does raise -- I mean, she speaks up, so she's able to make this cross-section point, which goes to, you say, there's some overlap. Should the judge there have paused to -- I mean, so is -- is the idea here that the judge should have paused and said do you have anything further to say to show that this was pretextual?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, there's sort of two junctures here within five, six pages of the JA that are important. The first are, after the race-neutral determinations are made, the court then immediately instructs the -- the defense to start striking the jury with the panel that's in the box. So, after that's done and before the venire is dismissed or as the venire is dismissed, the defense attorney returns to the issue and -- and seeks --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But did the defense attorney have an obligation to speak up even at that other point and at the first point you're describing and say this is why -- I mean, usually we expect lawyers to assert their -- their points, to assert their objections. So I'm just wondering, does the judge have an obligation to stop and elicit a response, or is it the defense counsel's burden to, because the -- the defendant bears the burden of proving the Batson challenge, right?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Barrett. In a situation where you have four strikes that are being addressed in succession and the court elects to deal with the step 2 all in a row, it would be reasonable and certainly kind of the only way to -- to encounter what's transpiring in that back-and-forth between the prosecutor and the judge to expect that step 3 will occur after that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But did defense counsel have an obligation to raise the point?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: The court has an obligation to conduct step 3 in the first place. The burden of persuasion remains with the defense counsel throughout all this, but it also coexists with a parallel duty that the trial court has to make its determination and critically --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So are you -- are you returning to the argument which was addressed -- the Fifth Circuit got to this point that you're saying that the trial court made no determination about whether there was a Batson finding?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Barrett. That -- that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So you're --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: -- what this record reflects, yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, I -- I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Mr. Perkovich --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- I think, if you look at Purkett, Hernandez, and Snyder --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- they answer Justice Barrett's question in that the obligation is on the trial judge to make a finding on step 3, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: To clarify, though, I -- I agree with that. I was just talking about the argument about the --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yes -- no, no, no. I -- my follow-up to that was I think that what you're saying, given the transcript, is I think the judge believed he only had to find a race-neutral reason because he says that at the very beginning of this process. He says: All I have -- at times, a lot of times on Batson, I just have the state give race-neutral -- I'm assuming he meant race-neutral reasons -- as to all.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And she says: I think the jurisprudence simply states the court must make a determination on the basis of all the relevant circumstances to racial discrimination. The judge then says: I'll have the state give race-neutral reasons. And after each race-neutral reason is given, he then asks for the next one and the next one and the next one. And I think your point is he never pauses to give her a chance to address pretext, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right, Justice Sotomayor. This --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Or for him to make the required third step finding.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: This is a -- this is an exchange between the prosecutor and the trial court for all four of the step 2 steps.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Mr. Perkovich, what happened here is certainly not a model to be followed in future cases, but I wonder if you would agree that in interpreting this transcript, we can take into account the way defense counsel generally behave in a situation like this.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, when -- if you could clarify that question of -- of defense counsel's general behavior.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, this is the most timid and reticent defense counsel that I have encountered. Any competent defense attorney that I knew would have spoken up. Let's take this -- you know, take this example. This is on 169, Juror Number 30, the prosecutor is called upon to give his race-neutral reasons. He says: She has mental problems according to a police captain, they've had numerous calls to her house, and said she obviously has mental problems. And then the -- the court says that would be race -- that would be race-neutral as to that juror. And nothing is said. I mean, any -- with respect to all of these jurors, all the defense counsel I have known would be standing up and say, Your Honor, that's a pretext and so forth and so on to make clear that -- that the point is driven across to the -- to the trial judge. I don't understand what happened here, unless -- I mean, unless -- well, anyway, I don't understand it.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, Justice Alito, I think the fact that this is sort of a two-person conversation very clearly from the record and that these strikes were clustered reflected what was going on in that courtroom with the judge just addressing the steps in succession. I think the reasonable expectation is that defense was going to be heard, and that -- and that third step just did not happen. Rather, the court instructed her to strike her panel. And she returned to this once -- once there was that opportunity that we've already discussed.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Yeah, but I know, you know, trial -- trial lawyers have to have a certain amount of toughness. And she had every opportunity. Now the judge didn't handle this the way it should have been handled.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: The judge should have said, okay, that's -- that's your reason; defense counsel, what -- what do you have to say, if anything? You know, that's what should have been done. But, really, she had every opportunity to make her point and she didn't. It's not -- it's not a case where the judge said shut up, sit down, I'm going to hold you in contempt if you say anything more.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, it didn't get to -- to that kind of pitch, certainly. However, the way this rushed process occurred throughout the whole morning, culminating in the strikes, as I was starting to answer Justice Barrett, in context, in Miller-El, voir dire took five weeks with a venire that was 108 people as opposed to 96. So the rushed nature of this, I think, informs how we make sense of this record. Another example of how rushed this was, because I -- I think the judge was very sensitive to the sequestered jury's hardship, the liability phase ended a day earlier than expected. And afterward, counsel sought to have a continuance of a day because their single expert was subpoenaed in Texas. The judge denied that and forced counsel to proceed without their -- their single witness.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Counsel, I appreciate all of that, and I -- I understand this was not a model by anybody's stretch of imagination. But I didn't see where the defense counsel even mentioned the word "pretext" to the court anywhere in the transcript until after trial.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And given that, could somebody read this as saying I don't have a pretext argument, you know? I mean -- and one of Ms. -- colleagues emphasized we're under D2, so we're not asking did she waive; we're asking whether the Mississippi Supreme Court could reasonably -- any reasonable jurist could reasonably conclude that she waived the argument by not mentioning even the word. How do I get -- help me with that.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: There's a fair amount to unpack there, Justice Gorsuch. I'll try with the waiver question as sort of the -- the end of this process. So before that is -- is a finding, a determination from the supreme court that there was a -- a -- a failure to rebut. And -- and, again, the effort is to be heard. The response from the court is this is clear in the record. And then the line's drawn under Batson and a shift is to the other issues. So the moment of truth for that was then. And, as Justice Sotomayor pointed out, in the motion for new trial, that was the first sort of opportunity in terms of the -- the basic procedure here to speak to that, which was done. Now the D2 point is -- is critical here, but also, there -- there is a D1 implication in this breakdown and -- because --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, help me with the D2 because that's what I'm struggling with at the moment.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: We have to decide whether the Mississippi Supreme Court made an unreasonable determination of fact. The determination of fact here is that she waived or, really, more accurately, forfeited. But it's her burden, we all agree on that, to -- to raise pretext. She didn't raise pretext at all, anywhere.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, Justice Gorsuch, if I may, so there is a burden of persuasion that -- that sticks with the challenger, the opponent of the strike, and that's the defendant. There's also a duty that the court has.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: I -- I appreciate --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: And that's first and foremost here.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: -- I appreciate that. I appreciate that.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: And -- and -- and that -- that duty's twofold, and that is to make a determination, irrespective of what's pressed by the advocate, and so there is a parallel sort of decoupling of it that is really important here.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: But you -- but you could find no Batson violation if there's no pretext argument made, right?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Respectfully, no. Actually, the -- the --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Really?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: -- the cases are quite clear in that regard.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: You don't -- if -- if -- if the plaintiff says -- sorry, the defense lawyer says, I -- I don't have a pretext argument, Your Honor?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, that would be --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Then there's no finding required on pretext, right?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Actually --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: You'd say there's no Batson violation, wouldn't you?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: The burden of persuasion's with the opponent of the strike, clearly. And if the strike is effectively expressly waived in that scenario, the -- the court still has --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: No, no, no, no, no, we have -- we have -- we have step 1.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Step 2 comes -- the government's lawyer comes up with race-neutral reasons. Step 3, suppose the defense lawyer says, I have no pretext argument, Your Honor.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Mm-hmm. Since the inquiry has made it to step 3, the trial court still has a duty.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Sure, to say no Batson violation basically.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: To -- to --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Isn't -- isn't the duty to look at the totality of the circumstances to determine whether or not the defendant has carried their ultimate burden of determine -- of -- of establishing discrimination?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That is -- yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So it may be -- it may be that with respect to that particular aspect of the back-and-forth, the defense counsel doesn't have an argument or they say, I don't have an argument. But that, I think, is not the sum total of the court's obligation to rule on the Batson objection. The defense counsel didn't withdraw the Batson -- the Batson objection.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: They just didn't make any argument with respect to pretext and --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: To be clear, I appreciate all of that. And I appreciate that the court has to make a determination. But the court here did make a Batson determination. He said: I find no violation. Now maybe that's wrong, okay?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And I -- I acknowledge it's a -- it's a muddled record, but -- but he did say I find no Batson violation at the end of it.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, the judge --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Now maybe that's insufficient, but that's not what's before us. What's before us is was there waiver.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And help me with the D -- help me with the D2 thing. I really want some help on that.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I'm trying, Justice Gorsuch, but the -- the key thing here is that his understanding from this record is of two steps. In other words, the equation is race-neutral equals no Batson violation. It completely elides the duty he has to do two things, right? Once the case shifts to step 3, the court has to afford an opportunity for the ultimate burden of persuasion to be met and make a determination, whatever is said in pressing that burden of persuasion. Those things exist in parallel because the Equal Protection Clause is implicated here, because there are concerns that are greater than the defendant's, the stricken venire member, the public's interest. And so all of that was jettisoned here, and -- and I think it's simply because he was unaware of it at least in this trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further?

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch asked you what would happened -- happen if the defense counsel said I have no pretext, no refutation -- no argument on pretext. And you said that there was more that the court was required to do in making the determination. What exactly is that? What do you mean by that?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: So, Your Honor, it's incumbent on the trial court to assess what is before the court.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: So what is before the court, there's -- you've got the initial assertion. You've got the race-neutral from the prosecutor. And you have a -- a defense counsel who says, I have nothing. So what else is there?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, you have a prosecutor who failed to question on the reasons that he put forward as his proffers for these strikes.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Were those challenged by the -- as -- were those challenged by the defense counsel as pretextual?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: As we've discussed, Justice Thomas, she did not speak to it after the step 2 proffers. And that's one of the failings in this record. There was not an opportunity after the step 2 proffers were -- were made and before the Batson challenges were overruled to speak to that.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: So you used Miller-El, which brings back a lot of bad memories.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: The -- you used Miller-El as your model, but didn't the defense counsel there challenge the race-neutral assertions?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Thomas, there's a radically different record in Miller-El from here as Miller --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Just -- the only portion I'm interested in is whether or not defense counsel made -- whether or not defense counsel there argued that this was pretextual.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: In -- in Miller-El, there were --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: -- there were days of opportunity to address each of the strikes. There was individual voir dire of each of the stricken members. There was a Batson hearing held dedicated to this whole inquiry.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Is that on the -- because the defense counsel challenged each of the strikes?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, no more than was done here, Your Honor. The challenge -- there's no question that the Batson challenge for the four strikes in question here was timely, never waive -- never withdrawn.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Let me ask you, we're -- I mean, we're focused on these strikes. Did the defense counsel make strikes, peremptory strikes?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: No, Your Honor. And -- and one reason for that is because -- well, the -- I misspoke. I misspoke. Please bear with me. No, they did. They certainly did.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: And how many?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I believe it was 11.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: And what were the races of those who were stricken?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I believe they were white. And you have to also realize that the venire was white for the balance of the strikes that they exercised, so they -- there was no other option.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: And how many -- how many were stricken by the prosecutor?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Seven.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: And how many -- what was the racial breakdown?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Three to four.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, I -- I want to understand exactly what you think has to happen in a situation like this. So the prosecutor -- the defense counsel says, I object to the peremptory challenge of this juror, and the trial counsel turns to the prosecutor and says: What is your reason? I think that a prima facie case has been made. What is your reason? The prosecutor provides a reason that's race-neutral and is a reason that almost every prosecutor would find is a good reason for peremptory strike -- peremptorily striking a juror such as here, one of the jurors, he has a brother that has been convicted of manslaughter, and considering that this is a murder case, I don't want anyone on the jury that has relatives convicted of similar offenses. So seems legitimate on its face. Now maybe it's not, but -- and so then the trial judge turns to defense counsel and says: Defense counsel, anything to say? And defense counsel says: Nothing to say.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well -- well --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Now what -- what about there? Is that in -- and then the judge goes on. Is -- is that a Batson violation?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, in that hypothetical where the court actually turns and solicits input, that would probably be a waiver on -- on -- on that scenario. What I want to point to with respect to the proffers and, you know, family members listed in the questionnaires as implicated in the criminal legal system, the important thing to keep in mind here is that if that was a sincere concern, it would -- it would strongly suggest there would be questioning about that because not only are the -- the various stricken venire members implicated in that but others.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Yeah, okay. I -- I understand all that.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Did defense counsel have the opportunity to question these witnesses -- I mean these jurors --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: -- on voir dire? So the defense counsel could say: Well, look, he's been very -- you know, he's happy to have these white jurors who have similar records. Then the judge has to make a finding. But, if the defense counsel doesn't say anything --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, there --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: -- what is the judge supposed to do?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, my -- my response to your last question was there was questioning during the group voir dire. There was no questioning at that point after the proffers were made. And -- and, again, very legitimate concern in principle, implications in the criminal legal system. However, if that's a sincere concern, there's going to be questioning about it. And I think, if we look at Miller-El, there's a classic example of why, and that is with the field strike in -- in Miller-El, you have somebody who --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, I -- okay, I understand. But your -- your -- your argument is that even in the example that I gave you where the judge says: Defense counsel, do you have anything to say about this? And defense counsel says: No, nothing to say. That that is not -- that there could still be a violation there?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: No, I -- I -- I'm conceding that --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: -- Your Honor. That -- that would.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: So then the question is, how do we interpret this record? Suppose that the judge doesn't actually say that, which the judge should, but suppose defense counsel, as Justice Barrett was hypothesizing could happen in some case, you know, defense counsel, and -- and nothing is said or there's a pause and nothing is said. Then what?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, if there's a pause and nothing is said, then that's a different record from what we have. To return to the -- the -- sort of these legitimate-seeming race-neutral reasons, again, the disinterest in questioning on any of the reasons that are offered there is -- has been repeatedly recognized by this Court as denoting a sham. In fact, that's language in -- in Miller-El and Snyder as well. And so, you know, it's not that that's not a in principle legitimate reason. It's that it's not explored because often, when it is explored, what comes to light in that process is that the family member really has no connection to the situation and it's not disquieting for the prosecution and they don't -- wouldn't use it as a -- as a reason.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Of course, yeah. But that all is triggered by defense counsel standing up and saying: Your Honor, I object, that's a pretext. The prosecutor has not applied that rule to white jurors.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, no.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: That's all that was required.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, I'm referring to the duty that the court has, whatever is advanced in terms of the burden of persuasion that the opponent has, the court still has to be aware of what has transpired in his courtroom and what has come forward in voir dire, what has not come forward in voir dire in terms of questioning via relevance of the proffers to the case, the absence of record basis. You have proffers here, mental problems, drug problems, there's nothing in the record on that. And so the court just takes that on face value, and that's not the job. The job is to consider all the circumstances and to discredit proffers that are put forward without a basis in the record. None of that happened.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: So -- so, if the prosecutor says: I -- I'm striking this juror because she has mental problems and the defense counsel says nothing in response, the trial judge has to say, well, wait a minute, you know, you're just telling me the police captain says that she has mental problems, they've been to her house many times. We have to have the police captain come in here, you know, even though defense counsel hasn't said one word, we have to have the defense -- the police captain come here and question --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: -- or the officers who made the -- you know, the visits to the house?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Is that what you're saying?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Alito, those officers were in the courtroom under subpoena first, so -- and, again, I return to the fact that --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: I'm just trying to understand -- I don't want to prolong this. I'm just trying to understand what you think had to happen here. And your first answer was, if defense counsel says nothing -- this is my understanding of your first answer. If defense counsel says nothing, nothing to say, Your Honor, the judge can simply say, okay, fine, I find that -- that it's not -- it's not a racially-based challenge.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: No, Justice Alito, as I've been trying to advance, there still is this duty for the court to consider all the circumstances bearing on racial animosity in this record and to make an assessment, whatever is advanced here. And, obviously, our -- our -- our cardinal sort of point here is that on this record, you have a defense counsel seeking to be heard on this, and the response from the court is your case is in the record and I'm going to reiterate my ruling.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Okay. All right. I -- I understand. Thank you.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Going back to that point, what's clear from the record here is, as soon as he exercises the challenge against the woman with mental problems, the prosecutor then says the next juror, Juror Number 3, she was Juror Number 2, and the court interrupts and says: That would be a race-neutral again reason as to that juror, meaning the mental juror. And the prosecutor immediately goes to S-3. So there's no pause there.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: None.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No pause between the four jurors and the race-neutral reason. So there's no chance for the defense attorney to say anything, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now you get to Juror Number 4, the same pattern, the -- I'm sorry, Juror Number 5, and then he gets to the last juror that's being discussed in race-neutral reasons, and he immediately says to her you have to start with jury selection.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Again, there's no pause to say, do you have a response? When you're saying to Justice Alito that defense counsel should have asked questions of the jurors, she was never given an opportunity, you're saying, because the prosecutor never did any voir -- voir dire of these jurors?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So she had no way of knowing that any of these things bothered the prosecutor, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That's right, Justice Sotomayor. As -- as I pointed out earlier, the proffers were not questioned. So the only questioning that this prosecutor did of consequence was to do death qualification, which resulted in the decimation of the black venire. So you have a concentration of questioning, JA 95 to 104, where, in rapid succession, he's questioning, I think, 37 different venire members, 28 of them black, to elicit responses that lead to cause strikes. That's the level of questioning that's going on in this record. There's essentially no individual voir dire at all. There are six individuals who are -- are questioned near the end of voir dire and it transpires on a single day.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So defense counsel can't be faulted for not knowing what the prosecutor was worried about --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- in asking questions. Now, going to the question that -- at no point did the justice say -- did the lawyer -- the judge here say, I'm doing step 3 in any way, that these were not pretext or that the -- that I find them to be both race-neutral and not race-based, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now that's your prong 1. And Justice Gorsuch asked -- I think was asking you whether the judge made an implicit finding.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The only -- where -- place I find that potentially is where he says, "For the reasons previously stated, first, the court finds there to be no" -- well, "all the reasons were race-neutral as to members that were struck by the district attorney's office, and so the, the court finds there to be no Batson violation." That's not an implicit finding on step 3 because he's still relying simply on step -- on -- on the race neutrality, correct?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Sotomayor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So there's not even an implicit Batson finding --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- under step 3.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes. I don't see how you can read that and -- and take away the suggestion of anything more than a determination of race neutrality. And, as I've tried to emphasize, there is a very big difference between race-neutral and credible. And that court has to make a credibility finding no matter what else happens in that courtroom.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Just on Justice Thomas's and Justice Gorsuch's questions, if the trial judge had said do you have any response and they'd said we have no pretext argument, that's the essential equivalent of withdrawing the Batson objection. At least that's my view.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But what happened here, I gather, your position is, and what Judge Mills, the district judge in the habeas case found was, you know, Judge Mills said perhaps Pitchford's counsel should have been more assertive, but the court will not fault them for failing to present specific arguments on pretext when the trial court appeared to have been resolute in its brusque determination that no violation had occurred. In other words, there was never an opportunity. Now that -- the question's whether that -- you know, whether the what happened in the trial court was an unreasonable -- whether it's unreasonable to find waiver, I guess, or how you analyzed the trial record, but the point is not that the trial counsel said, oh, I have no pretext argument, correct? Would you distinguish those two things?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Kavanaugh. So what we have is trial counsel is seeking to be heard, right, before it's too late, as the venire is being dismissed, exiting the courtroom. The jury's about to be empaneled. That's when she's seeking to be heard, and she's rebuffed three times by -- it's in the record. It's -- it's clear in the record there is no Batson violation and let's -- let's turn to the other issues, the fair cross-section issue. So she's striving to be heard, and then, ultimately, she's reasonably relying on the court saying it's in the record. And this is in the context where she raises Miller-El, which had been decided about six months before this trial, wherein, clearly, the ability to press a case on appellate review was the letter of the day. So she's relying on what the trial court is saying, that this issue's over and the trial is moving forward and opening argument is going to happen in a moment.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What do you think about the -- Judge Mills' comment, perhaps Pitchford's counsel should have been more assertive?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I think that -- I think that, first, that's a -- that's a not unfair criticism. We've all seen records where, you know, there's more hurly-burly, let's say, and -- and elbowing. But the point is that she timely raised the objection. That's her duty. There's not a continuing objection requirement. There aren't authorities supporting that. And she sought to be heard before it was too late. And then, at the next moment, as Justice Sotomayor pointed out, where she could address these issues in the motion for new trial, that was done, adequately so. It was fit for purpose in terms of the motion for new trial. So, if the judge was actually concerned about these issues, he had an opportunity to take the case back before it was up on appeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: I have a few clarifying questions -- excuse me -- about your position. One, you're not, are you, asking us to revisit Ford insofar as it said that a state has the flexibility to adopt procedural rules for the assertion of Batson objections?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, certainly, no. That -- that, I would point out, is dicta, though. Ford was a case where --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So you are asking us to say something different? Or -- or do you agree that states have the right to come up with rules of forfeiture even for Batson objections?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, certainly, the implementation's left to the states. And, as we've seen in Johnson versus California, where there was a step 1 issue that California had, there can be problems in that. And what we have here with respect to step 3 and this particular ruling on -- on waiver and the failure to rebut being the predicate for that is an example of faulty implementation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But you're not challenging the state's ability to set forfeiture rules and to say that if you don't make a timely objection, you forfeited it? I mean, I'm -- I'm asking you as a general rule. I -- I understand your argument that it would -- that she wasn't given a fair opportunity here. I'm not asking about that right now. I'm just saying you don't disagree, do you, with the fundamental proposition that a state can establish procedural rules requiring the timely assertion of Batson objections?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, the last formulation you gave I would agree with, certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: You would agree with that. Great.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: The very last formulation, though.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Thanks.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: You're welcome.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: The other question I have, it's a little bit difficult to tell at some points in your -- your brief, I -- I read your brief to maybe be taking the position that an appellate court has an obligation to conduct the Batson challenge, a Batson analysis, sorry, even if it wasn't made below. So, for example, that the Mississippi Supreme Court here actually had a duty to conduct the comparative juror analysis?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Well, I think the first point here is that the understanding of its own law for Batson was that it could not, right, and that it was unable to consider arguments pressed for the first time on appeal, especially in a context where the first opportunity to do that was on appeal. That's a problem. And so it's that fundamental misunderstanding of this Court's law and that basic gateway rule at the outset of the appeal that is the first problem with that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So what is your position, that -- that the -- that an appellate court has the discretion and should understand itself to have the discretion to consider it for the first time on appeal but not an obligation to do it?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, it's -- this -- this Court's holdings have relied repeatedly on comparison that's been pressed in the first place on appeal, and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But is it an obligation or do you just have the discretion? Just -- just pick one.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I'll -- Justice Barrett, I'll pick discretion with the caveat, though, that the language of this Court is a must, right? If we look --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So is it obligation or discretion?

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: The law sounds like obligation. It's just not how I read our precedents.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So you're asking for us to take those precedents, if there's any ambiguity in the precedents, sort of take them a step farther to say there's an obligation?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I think that the precedents have to be surmised from what the Court is doing and how it's getting to the results in terms of the holding for clearly established law. And what the Court has done routinely is take argument, comparative analysis that's really not viable in most trial settings and -- and hear that on appeal in the first instance because of the -- the concerns I was speaking to earlier.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Okay, okay. So do you think we have what we need if we were to conduct that comparative analysis now to find a Batson violation now?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I think, unfortunately, that was the ball game for the state supreme court, and -- and, there, they elected not to do that. They had -- in that fork in the road, they could have taken what was submitted on appeal and make a merits decision or remand it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So there was sufficient evidence in the record on the comparative juror point. The comparative juror analysis, there was -- it's your position that the record is there and the Mississippi Supreme Court could have, based on the record before it or that we could now, say that there was a Batson violation based on what was introduced about comparative juror analysis on appeal?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Justice Barrett, yes, it was incumbent on the supreme court to take that comparison analysis or remand it to the trial court to conduct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But is it your position that that comparative juror analysis is sufficient, that it was there and the Mississippi Supreme Court had what it needed to find a Batson violation based on what you had?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Barrett. And the court had done just that in other cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So I'd like to get your reaction to what I hear your argument as, and I just want to make sure I -- I've got it straight. That the making of a Batson objection at the beginning of this process actually triggers duties on both the parties' parts and the court's part, so, obviously, you make the objection and then we say at step 1 the defense counsel has the duty to establish whatever the prima facie case is. The burden then shifts. The duty becomes the state's to offer race-neutral non-discriminatory reasons. And then, ultimately, there is a duty of the court to actually resolve the objection, so -- the initial Batson objection. So, to the extent that people have asked what was supposed to happen here, I suppose, if we're in a world that Justice Thomas posits, if the defense counsel, when we get to step 3, the trial court says: Counsel, and defense counsel says: Your Honor, I have no argument on pretext, well, first of all, you would, I think, expect the court to say: So are you withdrawing your Batson objection, counsel? I mean, Justice Kavanaugh says, well, that's sort of implicit, but I think the court, because it has a duty to resolve the objection, has to be clear about whether this thing is still on the table once counsel affirmatively says I don't have any pretext evidence. So right there we have a problem with the court not clarifying whether this objection is still live in -- in the world of even asking. But -- but, in the world of not asking, one -- not asking the -- the defense counsel whether there's any pretext evidence, you would still have the court have to resolve the objection, right?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I mean, in -- in -- in -- in -- you -- you would expect the court to say something like: Hearing no evidence or argument related to pretext and seeing no basis in this record for determining that the prosecutor's race-neutral reasons were pretextual, I find that there is no race discrimination or whatnot, right? Like, the court has to make a finding --

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: -- that indicates that it's actually resolving the -- the objection on the basis of all of the evidence presented after all of the steps. And it seems to me that there's kind of like a problem here that the lack of clarity is a problem for the court because it suggests that the court did not fulfill all of its obligations triggered by the initial Batson objection. Does that comport with sort of what you're trying to say at least about what the court's duties were here?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes, Justice Jackson. Again, counsel has a duty to press the case and the ultimate duty or -- or burden of persuasion, but that's simultaneous with the duty that by the time this gets to step 3 in the burden framework, it remains with the court to do the things that you've outlined.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the court can say there's not evidence that there's discrimination here.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You haven't presented anything, et cetera. But, here, we have a court that's seeming to keep the defendant from making her presentation. So I don't understand how it could -- it, the court, could have fulfilled its obligation to look at all the evidence if it has prevented essentially one party from presenting. Let me just ask you another final question. I see this also as possibly a very short opinion when we look at what Mississippi's Supreme Court said. It would go something like Pitchford's trial counsel made a Batson objection and reraised it multiple times. Each time, the trial judge reassured her that it was preserved. Nevertheless, Mississippi Supreme Court said it was waived. That's unreasonable. The end. What would be wrong with that?

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Not -- not anything I can think of.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: I mean, we could say more because, with respect to D2, obviously, there -- there is a breakdown in -- in that fact determination that becomes a waiver under the Mississippi rule. With respect to D1, you know, another way to look at this is this is a case that -- that, you know, I don't think this Court has seen where it just sort of falls off the ledge at step 2. And so that's a D1 failing categorically. There's just an absence of that step being taken for the reasons you've outlined.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Stewart?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In Flowers versus Mississippi, this Court faced an extraordinary case and ruled against the state. This case is also extraordinary but in a very different way that requires a very different result. Start with the facts in D2. Petitioner claims that the state supreme court was objectively unreasonable in finding that he waived his pretext arguments. As Petitioner once admitted, that is wrong. Years ago, Petitioner declared that he failed to "properly challenge, litigate, and preserve" his pretext arguments. Petitioner also now claims that the trial judge thwarted his efforts to argue pretext. Years ago, he said the opposite. He declared that he "made no attempt to rebut or otherwise offer argument or evidence" on pretext. In short, Petitioner once declared that the facts are X. He now declares that it is objectively unreasonable to find that the facts are X. That is extraordinary. Now take the law. In Ford versus Georgia, this Court ruled the lower courts can adopt timely preservation rules to implement Batson. Many courts have done that and adopted a waiver rule like the one applied here. Petitioner ignores Ford -- Ford versus Georgia, he never cites it once, and he asks this Court to declare that a rule adopted by most federal courts of appeals is not just wrong but objectively unreasonable. That is extraordinary. Petitioner also claims that the state trial judge failed to conduct step 3 of Batson. That was the core of his presentation this morning. That defies what the trial judge said, and it once against defies what Petitioner has said. Years ago, Petitioner agreed that the trial judge conducted step 3. On direct appeal, he declared in his reply brief that the judge "made a final ruling of non-discrimination." That is step 3. That is page 484 of the Joint Appendix. He then ties that in a footnote to the same pages we cite here for that finding. As I said when I began, this case is extraordinary. Petitioner has conceded all that is central to his claim. Never has this Court granted relief under AEDPA when a habeas petitioner has so decisively doomed his own case. This case should not be the first. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: But counsel is arguing that notwithstanding what the defense counsel failed to do, the judge had a more active or more robust role to play. What's your reaction to that?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: No case of this Court says -- says that. No -- no holding of this Court clearly establishes that, Justice Thomas. What this Court -- Court's cases say is, at step 3 of Batson, the trial judge, if it reaches that stage, must make a finding on purposeful discrimination, whether the defendant has carried his burden. The judge did that here.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: The counsel also argues or at least uses Miller-El as a model. What was -- what was -- what is your reaction to what he said about Miller-El and what it requires?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I think Miller-El requires really no more than, on federal habeas review, state courts cannot make factual rulings that are objectively unreasonable. That's all Miller-El decided. It didn't reach the validity of a state procedural rule. It didn't rely or -- or cite or overrule or even touch Ford versus Georgia. It didn't reach any of those things at all.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: How would -- how would Petitioner here have preserved under Mississippi law the -- would not have waived these claims under Mississippi law?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I think it would have been quite easy, Justice Thomas. I mean, he could have said for -- for each of these challenges it would have just been two or three sentences, perhaps for Mr. Tillman, just one sentence. I mean, for Ms. Lee, who -- who was late, he could have said, Your Honor, I mean, other jurors were -- were late, you just refused to strike her for cause and there's no evidence of mental problems that should be explored. That would have preserved and unlocked the panoply of arguments that he later made about her in comparisons on appeal. He could have easily done that.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But didn't the court say it was preserved? I mean, she was obviously saying, I would like to make my record with respect to the Batson observations. So the Chief Justice points out she says, at some point, I would like to do that. So you just articulated the kind of thing you would have expected her to say, and I'm trying to figure out when she was supposed to be or given when she was supposed to have that opportunity.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Sure. At -- at page 169, at page 170, at 175, 176, she could have said that anywhere there, Your Honor -- Justice

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You mean --

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: -- Jackson.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: -- when -- when -- when the objections were happening?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Oh, yes. Of -- of course.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did she have to? Could she have done it after all of those objections were lodged?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Sure. She just had -- she had to do --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But -- but when she went to do that, when she -- when she tried to do that, she said at some point I'd like to do that, and the court said you've already done it. So whose mistake is that?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: He did not say you've already done it, Justice Jackson. What he said was you preserved your Batson objection --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: He said I -- he said I think you've already made those and they're clear in the record. And that's after another sentence where she adds -- she doesn't say "at some point." She then says, "I don't want to let the paneling of the jury go by without having those objections." And the court says, "I think you already made those, and they are clear in the record. For the reasons previously stated, the court finds there to be no -- well, all the reasons were race-neutral as to members that were struck by the district attorney's office. And so the court finds there to be no Batson violation."

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: She's trying to make the objections right there, and he says you already made them and there's no Batson violation, and he makes a legal error in the course of that by saying because they're race-neutral, there's no Batson violation, which of course is not the correct inquiry, I think you would -- you would acknowledge.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I think -- so two -- two responses on that.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So right there, she's trying, I think. I mean, she could have been more assertive. Judge Mills -- so your opening, like, was -- was forceful. Judge Mills is a very experienced district judge. He had been a former Mississippi Supreme Court justice. He knows what he's doing. He read the record entirely differently than you did. And he pointed to this -- this -- this part right here where she's trying, I think -- we weren't there; we don't have an audio recording -- to -- to make the objections, and -- and the court says you already made those, they're clear, and the court finds there to be no Batson violation. I don't know, at that point, she could have said, well, you're wrong, Judge. I get that. And Justice Alito has a good -- good points on that. But seems pretty clear at that point.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, she preserved her objection. She didn't preserve the pretext arguments, which are different than making a prima facie case or the objection itself. Comparative juror arguments are very different in response to the state's reasons than simply making a prima facie case. And she didn't raise those.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: You don't think at that moment right there she -- she's not prepared to do that?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean -- not at all, Your Honor. I mean, the very next page on page 176 --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I don't know. He says there is no Batson violation per se. And I don't know. I mean, that's where Judge Mills says perhaps she could have been more assertive. And I take that point, and that's why this is hard.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Well -- and I think -- and this would get back to, I think, you know, you -- you I think framed it correctly earlier, Justice Kavanaugh, where you said, look, is it objectively unreasonable, is (d)(2) met? And I think the ways we know that are a number of them. First of all, Ms. Steiner speaks only from page 158 to 176 of the transcript. During that time, she at least five times inserts arguments or objections without any prompting by the trial court. There's this talk about are there pauses in the trial court? There are no indication of pauses in here. Yet, five separate times, in addition to all the arguments she makes, she inserts herself. I mean, that was the Batson objection. They didn't -- you know, there's no indication that the judge paused, looked at her and, said, well, you make a Batson objection. She inserted that. She inserted the bench conference. I close the point by noting, at page 176, that the judge then did allow her to state the composition of the jury, her assertions about the composition of a county. And, you know, so she -- she had an opportunity to add more, and that's all she added, was bare numbers. Then we looked at post-trial motion. Even when she had days and days, all she came up -- and this was belatedly -- was the amended motion for a new trial, belatedly comes up with one sentence that points the judge to nothing about these comparisons.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But, General --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- in the course of -- go ahead.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, I think you made the point that she didn't do very well, you know, that she could have done a lot better than she did. But that's really not the question before us. The question is whether she's waived her objection. And three times, she's told by the court that the objection has been preserved. So you're right, General, she should have said a lot more stuff. She should have been more assertive. But the only question before us is, did she waive her objection when, three times, she's told by the court you're objection is in the record, I hear you?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And this is critical, Justice Kagan. The Mississippi Supreme Court did not hold that her objection was waived. It held that her pretext arguments were waived. It upheld the finding --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: That's slicing the baloney very thin.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: It's -- it's -- Justice

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I'm sorry, General Stewart.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: My apologies, Justice Kagan.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Here we are after the prosecutor has done all his here's my race-neutral reason, here's my race-neutral reason, and then the defense counsel says: I'm not persuaded, I need to -- you know, I need to say something about this. I still have an objection. Well, at that point, of course, she's objecting to the ultimate finding.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, she --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: This is after the prosecutor has given all his race-neutral reasons, and she's still objecting. So what could she be objecting to at that point?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Oh, I agree --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: She must be objecting to the fact that either the race-neutral reasons aren't credible or they're -- you know, or they're -- or they're -- they're pretextual. That's what she's objecting to.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: But --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: There's no other way to read the context of this colloquy without saying that at that point she's objecting, she's saying what the prosecutor gave you wasn't enough.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I agree that she is maintaining her Batson objection, Your Honor. I don't agree about the comparative juror pretext arguments that she made no suggestion of making despite many opportunities to do so.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I think you're arguing this on the merits. I think you're saying she didn't say enough to allow the court to find for her. But that's not the question before us. The question before us is whether the -- the state supreme court was right when it said she waived her argument. She might not have presented a good argument, but she didn't waive her argument.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: She -- and -- and, respectfully, Justice Kagan, the Mississippi Supreme Court didn't say she waived the argument writ large. It said she waived -- she waived the pretext arguments that she never presented to the trial judge. And that's critically different. Justice Graves said in dissent, my friend -- my friend in state federal habeas petition, agreed that the Mississippi Supreme Court resolved the claim on the merits. It held that the Batson pretext arguments, the comparative juror arguments that she had never made to the trial judge, that's what she waived. That's what she failed to --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, let me just follow up on Justice Kagan's questions. After the prosecutor gives the asserted race-neutral reasons, what else could she be objecting to than those were pretextual?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I -- I think what we -- I mean, she -- I think she's saying, like, I -- I believe that I've made my case. I mean --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: No, no, no. Answer my question. After the prosecutor has given the asserted race-neutral reasons, what else could she be objecting to other than that they were pretextual?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I mean, I think she's -- are you talking about page 175, Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Okay. I think there she's just making sure that her objection is preserved in the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What else that's after the asserted -- just answer my question -- after the prosecutor has given the asserted race-neutral reasons, and she goes back to Batson, at that point what else could she be objecting to other than that the asserted reasons were pretextual?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think it's the ultimate conclusion the district judge reached or -- pardon me -- the trial judge reached, that they were -- that they were race-neutral, in fact, and she hadn't shown discrimination. I mean, I think that --- that's why I think she -- she uses the word "reserve." And I think she wants to make sure -- I mean, that's -- that's the 175. She says I'd like to reserve my --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then she -- then that's the first sentence. The second sentence, I don't want to let the paneling go by without having those objections and then repeat myself. I think you've already made those, says the judge, and they were race-neutral. Can I ask a separate distinct question, just one question, which is in the course of that paragraph, the court says -- and this is now (d)(1) question --

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The court says, the trial court, "all the reasons were race-neutral as to members that were struck by the district attorney's office, and so the court finds there to be no Batson violation." Is that a legally correct analysis of Batson?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: It -- it is in that it means that he is finding the actual -- the reasons to be race-neutral in fact. Like, he is --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But is that the end of the Batson analysis, in your view?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean if -- if he's finding them to be the actual reasons, the race-neutral reasons, in fact, then yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Now, if they're -- if they're race-neutral, is that the end of the Batson analysis?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: If -- if it's just -- if the question is just are they facially race-neutral, then that's step 2, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right. And that's not the end of the Batson analysis, correct?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: The -- whether there's a facial justification it's race-neutral is not the end of the Batson inquiry.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: What -- what I'm --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That's -- Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: But whether in the end they are race-neutral is the question, right?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: And as to what she was objecting to, if she wasn't tacitly raising a pretext argument, what she said on 176 is "Allow us to state into the record there is one of 12 -- of 14 jurors -- are non-white, whereas this county is approximately, what, 40 percent?" What she's -- what she's saying is that the racial makeup of the jury that was selected was so starkly different from the racial makeup of the county that that would be sufficient to find that there were Batson violations.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Right -- that's right, Your Honor. And actually in the cert petition at page 38, the Petitioner emphasized this point as being part of the Batson --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, I don't know how that could be. She starts her colloquy in that section by saying I want -- at -- at some point the defense is going to want to reserve both its Batson objection and a straight for Tenth Amendment racial discrimination. That reference, I -- I don't think it was on the Tenth Amendment. I think what she meant was the -- the earlier objection that they spent a lot of time in voir dire on, on the cross -- whether the jury pool was a representative body, correct? There had been two objections.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Two objections there, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. So she's talking about the cross-reference -- the representation argument and the Batson argument. On the Batson argument, the court starts with that and says: You have already made it in the record so I'm of the opinion it is in the record. She says, I don't want to let the paneling of the jury go by without having those objections. And the court thinks -- I think you already made those. And they are clear in the record for the reasons previously stated, he says, all the reasons were race-neutral, blah, blah, blah. And then as to the other issue, the other issue is the jury pool issue, oh, I've already ruled based on prior rulings from this -- the United States Supreme Court and the state of Mississippi that jury selection was appropriate. That's -- that the jury itself, the pool, was appropriate. And those are noted for the record. It is then when she starts talking about the jury pool as a whole, that as a whole and not on the Batson question.
