So I'm not quite sure that you're fairly representing her use of that one example or their colloquy on that one example.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Let me cite you something that shows it is -- it is a fair representation. It's page 38 of the cert petition. That's Petitioner's filing where he ties that very exchange to his preservation of his Batson claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But, counsel, I -- I think in fairness, Justice Kavanaugh asked you what more could she have done or why wasn't she, you know, responding to or trying to say that there's pretext when she was talking about the Batson question. You pointed to this 40 percent issue that she talks about, the 12 of 14 jurors, but as Justice Sotomayor points out, that discussion was in reference to the jury composition issue and not the Batson issue. So regardless of what was happening in the cert petition, I'm talking about what's going on in the trial record in the discussion that they were having. It looks as though the court resolved the Batson question by not giving her a chance to make any more arguments or say anything more about it, and then he says, the court, then as to the other issues, and he moves on to composition, and that's when she starts making statements about 12 of 14 jurors are non-white. So I think we have to be clear about what's actually happening in the record and not ascribe to her representations that actually don't fit.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Can I make -- make two quick responses to that, Justice Jackson? One is my friend conceded this morning that that point went to her trying to get to the Batson claim itself. And also --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Okay. Well, that's just not what the -- what the transcript says.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: But Your Honor, we're here on AEDPA relief. And the question is whether a reasonable fact finder could conclude this.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: No, I'm not talking about the question. I'm talking about what the court said they were talking about at that moment. He resolved the Batson issue and then he says: And then as to the other issues, and there were two issues, and the other issue was about jury composition. And that's the one that she responds to with the relevant data.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And respectfully, I don't agree. He conceded otherwise, Your Honor, and --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: -- it would also -- and she did it without prompting. There was -- there was no, you know, would you like to say more? I mean, she said the more that she wanted to say. And the key thing is even when she had that opportunity, she did not present the comparative juror argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I don't think it helps -- I don't think it helps to -- to -- to misrepresent what the record obviously shows. Let me ask you another question because it seems as though the Mississippi Supreme Court is trying to have it both ways with respect to the significance of her arguments related to pretext. Is -- is -- is it your position that the argument -- that her argument was so central that the failure to present it waives any obligation of the court to rule on whether the reasons that the state has given a race-neutral and, therefore, there's no Batson violation here? Because she didn't say anything about it or, as you interpret the record, that alleviates the court of any obligation to say, that's one thing that I think the Mississippi court is saying, but, on the other hand, they also say it isn't necessary for the court to give her a chance. So either it's central, in which case the court had to give her a chance, or it's not central, in which case the court had to rule on it, I think, in -- regardless. So which is it?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I'm not sure I'm completely following the question, Justice Jackson. What I -- what I would say --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What is the significance of her argument related to pretext? Is it so necessary that without it, the court is relieved of any obligation to continue to look at the facts and to actually make a finding or is it not necessary, in which case it seems to me that the court needed to do that regardless of the fact that she didn't bring any argument about it.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Right. So I -- I think when you're -- if you get past step 2, the -- the court has to make an ultimate finding on non-discrimination. But it does so in light of the party's submissions. No -- no precedent of this Court requires the judge to consider matters, especially intricate competitive -- compare -- comparative juror arguments that are not presented to it. I mean that's something this Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And your view is that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you. Justice Jackson?

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I just wanted to say, and your view is that if the court has to do its obligation in light of the parties' evidence, the court does not have the obligation to turn to her and say what is your evidence about this? The court doesn't have to solicit the evidence from this party or give her an opportunity to present it?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Well, doesn't have to affirmatively do that. There's every indication that in the trial -- in the record here and a reasonable person could certainly read the record to allow multiple opportunities to present that. There's nothing supporting the idea that the trial judge cut her off or certainly nothing compelling that view of the record, Justice Jackson.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further?

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Yes. General Stewart, would you -- you were about to discuss the role of AEDPA in our review, much of this is being discussed as though we're on de novo review. So would you discuss what AEDPA requires?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Sure, Your Honor. And I think we've been very focused on the (d)(2) piece so I'll -- so I'll -- I'll lead with that. There have been various discussions about, you know, could the record be read this way, are there pauses that allowed her to -- you know, to step in, did the trial judge actually turn to her and give her this opportunity. You know, I heard lack of clarity. All those things mean that the -- that the Petitioner can't be granted relief under (d)(2). The record has to compel a finding that the trial judge failed on some factual matter and it just cannot possibly compel that finding. As I've emphasized, I mean, Petitioner himself took a very different view of the -- the record and he now says the view that he took is objectively unreasonable. And I think that goes for the legal matters too, Your Honor. There has to be something objectively unreasonable here. And the record can be read certainly either of two ways. That would be enough on clear error review, Anderson versus Bessemer City. It's certainly enough for the state under AEDPA. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito? Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You seem to be suggesting that the only way that we could overturn the Mississippi on AEDPA is if the district court had said to her: Sit down, counsel, you made your Batson challenge, period. Anything short of that, they couldn't err in finding a way -- a non-waiver -- a waiver?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean given the entirety of this record there's -- there's no

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no, no. I'm saying your argument seems to be that only if the trial court said counsel, sit down, Batson has been preserved, I don't want to hear anymore, that that's the only way that on AEDPA review we could say Mississippi made an unreasonable determination of fact?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, again, given -- given this record, I think you would need something like that.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Where have we ever said that a waiver has to be that explicit? Don't we infer it from evidence?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I mean, even if that were true, Justice Sotomayor, this gets back to --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, even if it's true, AEDPA, and unreasonable, doesn't mean that all interpretations of a record are correct. I would like you to point me to one part of this record, one word by the court that says do you have something more on race neutrality?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: But that's exactly the point, Justice Sotomayor. She has none of that. And she has multiple indications that she could have --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, the problem --

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: -- that she could have -- she could have asserted --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- is that she was never given an opportunity on this record.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The way the record -- well, we can go -- I'm not going to argue with you on this point. The record speaks for itself.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: May I respond just briefly? I think I could say something helpful on this, Justice Sotomayor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no, no. I was going to ask one last question, okay, on the preservation issue -- not on the preservation issue, on the comparative jury analysis on appeal, the comparative jury analysis on appeal. In answer to Justice Barrett's question, appellate courts can do their own comparative juror analysis even if not preserved below, correct?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: They can.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's what happened in Miller-El and Snyder. Do you believe the Mississippi Supreme Court says it cannot consider those arguments because of the waiver or that it's choosing not to consider them?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: It's -- it's saying that in general it does not. It can exercise its discretion to do so. We know that --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It could do so?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: It could do so.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So is that a plain error standard? Because plain error is what federal courts use to review evidence that has not been presented -- an objection that has not been preserved below. Could it -- it would be plain error, correct?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, that's what the -- that's what has been said in the case. I mean, Flowers versus State III, the Flowers case, I mean, what the Court said was, because the error in upholding the strike of Juror Pitman affects a substantial right, we apply the plain error rule to find a Batson violation occurred. So, I mean, we had the question --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So Mississippi has a similar plain error rule? It has discretion to overlook a waiver, correct?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: It has discretion.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So, if the record is clear that there was a Batson violation -- and we can argue about each individual juror whether the record is clear or not -- I want you to make an assumption, if the record is clear that there was a Batson violation, would the Mississippi court have erred in failing to -- to conduct that review?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And the answer is no, certainly not under AEDPA.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, not under AEDPA? It's a constitutional violation in a case where the court below did not make an actual step 3 analysis. You're saying that it wouldn't have been error?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, that's -- that's consistent with the waiver or forfeiture of constitutional objections generally and with jury objections in particular.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Oh, that -- that -- we apply plain error.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And -- but state courts don't necessarily have do that. They can do -- do pure discretion. They can do different approaches. Nothing in this Court's cases bar that, Justice Sotomayor. And, I mean, I would just emphasize that this is -- this is a rule that has been embraced by most federal courts of appeals, and it's one that was surely reasonably applied here.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yes, because we apply plain error.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Some -- some do; some don't. And, again --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yeah, you always have to apply plain error.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: That's not --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Plain error is required by the Rules of Federal Procedure. If an objection has not been preserved, the court has to determine whether the error was harmless or -- or --

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And I -- I'm sorry, Justice Sotomayor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- well, that the error was harmless.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: May I -- may I respond to that one, Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I -- I come back to Ford versus Georgia, where the Court affirmed that state courts can adopt these timeliness rules. Perforce, those rules are going to block some claims, perhaps even some meritorious claims. And given that signal, it was surely reasonable to adopt this rule, which ultimately vindicates and enforces Batson itself and makes for better Batson rulings, as we --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: General, I want to take you back to the conversation that you and I were having and that you were having with Justice Kavanaugh, which is just this question of waiver, because I do believe that, given the Mississippi Supreme Court decision, that's the only question before us. The question is not whether this defense counsel put enough in the record to actually prevail on a Batson claim. The question is instead whether the Mississippi Supreme Court got it really wrong when it said that she had waived her argument that there was a Batson violation. And, again, I'm just staring at this transcript, and I guess, as to that question, I don't really see the ambiguity in this transcript because this is coming after the fact of the prosecutor saying we had race-neutral reasons for all of them, and she says three times, I want to contest that. And the court says over and over, it's in the record, it's in the record, it's clear in the record. And when a court says that to you three times, I actually don't believe that it's, like, I'm not going to say what can you do? I think she could have done more. I think she could have said: Judge, really, like, I'm not moving on to anything else until I get this out. But, on the other hand, it's hardly ineffective assistance of counsel to make a different kind of choice, to say: I don't want to antagonize this judge. If he's insisting on moving on, I better move on. That probably was a wrong decision. I'll say that that definitely was a wrong decision, but that's not the question before us. The question before us is only whether she waived her objection. And how could she possibly have waived her claim that there was a Batson violation when she says it three times and the court says three times it's in the record, you've done it?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Okay. I appreciate that, Justice Kagan. I'd -- I'd -- I'd make two points. One is I really have to emphasize it's not a holding that she waived the objection or the claim. It's a holding that she waived the pretext arguments, and that's a critical difference.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: It's a -- I'm saying it's -- that what they said -- the pretext argument is just a way of establishing a Batson violation. So, if you keep on saying there's a Batson violation here, then you're saying there's something wrong with what the state has told you about their race-neutral justifications. And if you tell me that she had to use the word "pretext" in what she was saying, I'll tell you that's not the standard.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And what I'm saying, Your Honor, is that she had to give the judge a chance to rule on the comparative juror arguments that she later claimed were central to her argument. She did none of that. She didn't give the judge a chance to rule on it.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. In terms of giving people a chance, the judge had every -- the judge is the judge. The judge has every chance in the world to rule on whatever she wants to do. The judge could have said: You know, okay, I'll put you to it. Show me what you got. So you don't have to give the judge a chance.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, Judge, I -- I -- Justice Kagan, I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: And the judge said it's all in the record, it's all in the record, it's clear.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: No, respectfully, he -- he didn't say that, Justice Kagan. He -- he agreed that the objection was in the record. And all she said was, I want to reserve the objection. And he said it's -- you know, that's what he gave her a signal to. What -- what I'd emphasize, Justice Kagan, is, I mean, look --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: You think that the judge thought that -- that she was only objecting in the same way she did at the very start of the -- of the inquiry, that she was just making, like, a prima facie case? I mean, obviously, the judge did not think that. The judge thought that she thought that there was a Batson violation, and she said -- and he said, enough, it's in the record.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And -- and here's -- here's the key point, Justice Kagan. Whenever my friend or any hypothetical needs to suggest the judge cut her -- cut her off, it always has to use words that are not in the transcript. And she referred not to pretext, not to the desire to make more arguments. She didn't say, you know, Your Honor, I didn't make some arguments that I would like to make, can I just take a moment to preserve those? And every indication of this record is that he would have allowed her to. I mean, he allowed her to make an extended argument challenging death qualifications even though she conceded it was doomed. I mean, she talked about carpool routes and juror medical issues and jurors being married to each other. When she wanted to say something, she did. And the judge heard her out and very often ruled for her. In this part of the transcript, just like when -- when the actual Batson challenges in step 2 and step 3 were occurring, she didn't say any of that and didn't give any indication, and she still didn't do so in her posttrial motions.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: So I -- that's what I'd emphasize, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch?

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Briefly, I hope. Should you lose, what is the appropriate remedy? Let's say you lose on the (d)(2), so there's an unreasonable determination of fact. I know you're going to want to fight the hypothetical, but please don't.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Very good.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: We --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: If this Court would find there's an unreasonable determination of fact, but under 2254(a), of course, we can only grant a writ if we find a violation of the Constitution. So there's obviously more work to be done after we decide the waiver issue. Can you explain how that's going to proceed?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean, I think, number one, there would need to be a showing of a substantive, I think, Batson violation, potentially, overcoming (e)(1), for example, in district court. If this -- if -- if -- if this Court or the lower courts were to think that that -- that law and justice were -- were shown in this case to warrant issuance of the writ, I mean, I think it would go back to the state courts. I mean, this -- this would depend if the Court finds a substantive Batson violation, I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, no, we're not -- we're likely -- assume we're not going to make that inquiry, that we're going to confine ourselves to what we spent the last two hours talking about, which is the waiver issue, okay, and we find an unreasonable determination on the waiver issue. What more does a federal court need to do?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Got it. I mean, I -- I think, as we've said in our papers, the possible remedy in that case when it goes back down the federal chain would be a writ that would require release if the Mississippi Supreme Court does not in a certain amount of time undertake whatever analysis this Court holds to be lacking. So, if that's do a new step 3 analysis in light of the relevant arguments, that would be the -- the measured remedy there. I think that would be consistent, for example, with what's appeared when, say, habeas is granted for -- on a question of whether a confession was voluntary. You don't necessarily need to do a retrial -- retrial. You just need to see if the confession was voluntary. If it is, things can stand. I think similarly here, Justice Gorsuch.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: A couple things. First, I agree with you that the court does not need to do anything at the point in the transcript we've been focused on, defense counsel had said, I withdraw my Batson objection or, having heard the asserted reasons, I have nothing on pretext. So I agree with you in that situation. But that's, obviously, as we've explored, not what I -- it looks like it happened here. You -- you've said that she could have jumped in again after that colloquy on 175 after she twice raises it, but, at the end of that, the district court says, "The court finds there to be no Batson violation." Now, again, as Justice Kagan said, could have jumped in, but at that point, he said you've made the objections, you preserved them, and there's no Batson violation. Right?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Agree with that framing because, you know, again, made the objection, Your Honor. I think that's the critical thing.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right. And then, for why -- there are two possibilities for why I think that it got truncated here, which we've all explored. One is that the district -- the trial judge had a misunderstanding of what Batson required, didn't understand the third step, and that's in that sentence that I read before. Well, all the reasons were race-neutral as to members and so there's no Batson violation. Right? So that's one possibility. The other possibility, and Judge Mills in the federal habeas district court alludes to this, because Judge Mills applies AEDPA, correct?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: He says he does. I --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: -- I agree with that.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah. No, but he -- he articulates the AEDPA framework. He's a very experienced district judge, been a Mississippi Supreme Court justice. He says he's applying AEDPA unreasonable. And what he says happens is it stopped after two steps, simply put, no state court, whether it be the majority in the Mississippi Supreme Court or the trial court, conducted a full three-step Batson inquiry. And then he explains what he thinks could have happened. He's generous to the trial court on this, I think. The trial court, seemingly eager to proceed in the case itself, quickly deemed the races -- reasons as race-neutral and moved on. The trial court's actions, perhaps understandable and relatable to this court -- speaking to Judge Mills, trying to show empathy, I think, for the burdens on a trial judge -- are error nonetheless. So I think, you know, whether the trial judge misunderstood the third step or the trial judge was just, you know, rushing it along to try to keep things moving or whatever, it just never happened.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That's Judge Mills, again, applying AEDPA or at least articulating it, but I agree with Justice Thomas completely. We have to look through the -- the AEDPA standards here.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And -- and so I appreciate that, Justice Kavanaugh. To -- to take the -- the second bit first, I would come back to Petitioner's own words where he declared on direct appeal that the trial judge did decide step 3. And this is surely a fair reading of that record. That's page 484 of the transcript. And he, again, links that to the same parts of the transcript that we do in Footnote 4 on page 484.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: And I -- I -- I think that's quite important. I think it shows that it's not objectively unreasonable to find that the trial judge did conduct step 3 here. Could it have used better, more clear words? I think, you know, of -- of course, that's true, but what I think the judge was really doing was he wanted to be clear. I am finding these race-neutral in fact. I think that is what he was doing. That is surely a permissible view of the transcript. And, therefore, on clear error and, certainly, on AEDPA review, I think it is the compelled view in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Last question. Sorry to prolong it. Obviously, focused on this, but the reason this matters is this is a -- a death penalty case, right, and -- and he was 17 when he committed the crime, and he did not, not that this is good, but he was not the shooter, correct?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. I mean, not the fatal shooter, that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: General Stewart, I just want to understand your answer to Justice Gorsuch on remedy. So, if we think that the Mississippi Supreme Court was wrong on the forfeiture/waiver point, I don't understand how it gets back. You said something about it getting, you said this in your brief, something about it getting back to the Mississippi courts. Wouldn't the remedy be that we send it back down below for then the federal courts to analyze whether there was a Batson violation based on the comparative juror evidence?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I mean --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: I just don't see the route of bouncing back. It's not direct review.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Well, I -- I guess what I'm -- so it would not be -- we didn't suggest remand to state court straight from --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: -- this Court, certainly, Your Honor. I -- I think we're responding to the suggestion that the writ should issue here at all. I mean, my -- my friend has maintained that the writ should flat out issue ordering release or retrial. And our view is that if a federal district court is issuing the writ here, the most it can do is an order that would correct the actual violation, which, by hypothesis here, is not conducting a full step 3 analysis. And that could be something that is resolved by the state courts and would eliminate the constitutional amendment.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So you're saying the district court shouldn't -- if -- if we sent it back down -- I agree, we did not grant cert on the Batson question, so that's not for us to decide. But, if we sent it back down, you don't think that the district court, you know, or -- or, frankly, the Fifth Circuit -- I mean, he said that the court has what it needs in the record to look at the comparative juror analysis. Why couldn't the federal courts make the Batson determination themselves?

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: I think that would also be an option. I mean, I think, if the -- if you sent it back down to the Fifth Circuit and said, hey, look, you know, you were wrong to find waiver, I mean, I think it would have everything it needs in the Fifth Circuit to just flat out rule on the Batson violation. As -- as we said, you obviously know the merits of our position, Your Honor, on that one.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah, of course. Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson, anything further? Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: MR. STEWART
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Ms. Ferguson. SUPPORTING THE RESPONDENTS

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: State and federal appellate courts routinely decline to consider claims, issues, and arguments that were not preserved in the trial court, consistent with historical tradition. The Batson context is a particularly important time to apply that rule. Whether a Batson violation has occurred is fundamentally a question of fact. Requiring parties to preserve their arguments that a prosecutor's stated reason is pretextual ensures that trial courts can consider all of the relevant information as they make the factual determination whether discrimination has occurred, and it allows prosecutors to explain their strikes and decisions not to strike when the reasons are top of mind. Justice is better served when these questions are aired before the jury is sworn, when any errors can be fixed, rather than through a cold record on appeal. The Mississippi Supreme Court did not unreasonably apply federal law or unreasonably determine the facts when it determined that Petitioner failed to preserve his Batson pretext arguments in the trial court. This Court should affirm. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, Ms. Ferguson, would Petitioner have preserved this argument, the Batson arguments, if he had been in federal court?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: No, Petitioner would not have, I think, preserved the arguments. The federal courts would apply plain error and consider those arguments under that standard.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What do you do with the points we've been talking about on page 175, where the defense seems to be looking down the road for something that's going to happen, said at some point, I'm going to want to reserve these questions. And the judge does say: You know, you've -- you've already made those, they're clear in the record. In other words, his response, the judge's response to I'd like to reserve them is that you have.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well, Mr. Chief Justice, I think that that was likely a demonstration by defense counsel that she wanted to say more. And she, in fact, did say more. And so that -- I'd like to respond to something that was of significant discussion during the state's argument, which is that reference on JA 176 to the statistics. And the fair cross-section requirement applies to the venire, not to the selected jury. So, when the defense counsel is referring to the number of black jurors who are on the selected jury, not on the venire, that necessarily is an argument in support of the Batson claim and -- and shouldn't be an argument in support of the fair cross-section claim because she's talking about the selected jury. And so I think the -- the suggestion that that was only about the fair cross-section claim and not about the Batson claim just doesn't make a ton of sense. And I think it shows that she was, in fact, given the opportunity to raise additional Batson arguments and she chose not to raise any -- any argument as to jurors.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: So you're -- you're saying when she said, at some point, I'm going to want to reserve this, that that was the half page of the record later?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: I -- I do think that that's when she took the opportunity. And she could have said, you know, if I may, Your Honor, I have additional arguments to make. She didn't do that either before she said I'm going to want to make or after she said I'm going to want to make.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: In the federal courts, they already have plain error review. And counsel for the state tells us that the state also has the discretion to consider a plain error, correct?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. So if the juror comparison is clear from the record that there was a Batson error, that could be reviewed and should be reviewed by the Mississippi court. No?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well, I think two points on that. One is, you know, just the existence of an error being plain is not sufficient to make mandatory plain error review. And so under state law, the state court had discretion to consider that argument or not, depending on the other prongs. And the --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If the step 3 analysis was never done by the court. How could it not be an abuse of discretion to fail to have done -- done it by someone?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It's a judicial obligation to do a step 3.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: It is a judicial obligation for the court to engage in step 3, but I don't think it's an accurate depiction of the record to say that the trial court here did not make a step 3 finding.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I -- I know. We're going back and forth on that point.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: And I -- I'd add one more response on that --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you, counsel -- oh, go ahead.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: I would add one more response on that as to existence of a step 3 finding here, and that's on -- in this Court's decision in Purkett, the Court said that the state court had found at step 3 that the prosecutor was not motivated by discriminatory intent. And the -- the way the Court describes the facts in that case is that the state trial court, without explanation, overruled Respondent's objection and empaneled the jury. So we think that's very similar to what happened here. And the Court found that there was, in fact, a step 3 finding even though there was no explicit "I find the prosecutor's stated reasons to be credible."

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: How could there be a step 3 finding if usually that's evaluating whether the race-neutral reason is the true reason or whether instead it was pretext? If there had a has been no argument that it has been pretext, how could there be a step 3 analysis? That's point 1. And, point 2, Judge Mills, who evaluated this, the federal habeas court, flat-out said multiple times it ended at step 2.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I don't think that Judge Mills was -- was right to say that it ended at step 2. And I -- I know you pointed out earlier the state court's line that all the reasons were race-neutral. I think that saying all the reasons were race-neutral, to Justice Alito's point, demonstrates that the trial court at least implicitly had found those to be the actual reasons. You don't have a situation where the trial court said the prosecutor stated race-neutral reasons, and so we find there to be no Batson violation. That would look like a state court that was misunderstanding what Batson requires at step 3, if the -- if the state court said that only stating race-neutral reasons was sufficient. But here you had the -- the state court say all of the reasons were race-neutral. And I think that's enough, particularly given what this Court found in Purkett, to show that there was an implicit credibility finding there.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What -- what about the court saying all of the reasons were race-neutral and so the court finds there to be no Batson violation?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Justice Jackson, I think the answer is the same, and that's because, again, if the reasons are race-neutral, that implies that those are the actual reasons. And that's distinct from saying the prosecutor has stated --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Isn't that collapsing 2 and 3? I mean, 2 is race-neutral reasons; 3 is are they pretextual. And we've said you aren't supposed to collapse those two. So your argument just did so by saying if you have 2, then you've satisfied 3.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Justice Jackson, no, I don't think that that's our --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Didn't you just say if they're race-neutral, that means they are the real reasons?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: I -- I think the actual reasons are race-neutral, so not whether the stated reasons are race-neutral. If the prosecutor has merely stated race-neutral reasons and the trial court doesn't credit those, those reasons, or if there's any reason to believe that those are not the real reasons that the prosecutor exercised the strikes, then you can go --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Isn't there also a more complex way of looking at this that Miller-El brings up, which is not reason by reason but sort of the -- the comparison, right? There's no indication here that this court did any sort of comparative work, which would have been what we would have expected from the district -- I mean from the defense counsel if given an opportunity, correct?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: I think certainly you -- given what the -- what defense argued on appeal, you would have expected that.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Right. So if -- if the comparison is also a part of the determination of pretext, how does this record indicate that the court did any of that kind of analysis?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well, I don't think there's any particular indication that the court was sua sponte considering arguments that weren't raised, but I think that when it comes down to the ultimate question this Court has explained several times, the ultimate question is whether the prosecutor or the -- the striking party was engaged in intentional discrimination. And, necessarily, if the judge finds that the reasons were race-neutral, then race-neutral reasons, in fact, being exercised is incompatible with a finding of intentional discrimination.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Can I ask you a broader question? And then I'm done. The United States has entered this case as an amicus curiae, meaning that you did not have to be here. And as Justice Kavanaugh pointed out, this case is a capital case involving the conviction of a defendant who was not the shooter, a felony murder scenario. And so I guess I'm trying to understand the United States' interest. Your -- your -- your brief doesn't really pay a lot of attention or devote a lot of time to defending the actual conviction. So what -- what is the principle that you are seeking to have upheld in this case in which it's clear that the defense counsel said several times I have a Batson objection and tried to preserve it, and the court, if anything, misled her into believing that she had preserved it by saying it's in the record? Why is the United States interested in being involved in this case?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, the United States is a party in many trials, including many criminals trials, and is subjected to Batson objections and defense in Batson cases. And so I think our interest is in defending the right of courts of appeals to exercise their discretion to consider juror comparison arguments and -- and not as, I think, Petitioner, if I may, Your Honor --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Not as Petitioner argued in Petitioner's brief, although it seemed to -- to walk away from today, adopt a rule that appellate courts must consider juror comparison arguments when they are raised for the first time on appeal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice Alito? Justice Sotomayor? Justice Kagan?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Ms. Ferguson, I -- I want to go back to what you were saying before, and I'll -- I'll grant you something. I'm going to assume your view, that the judge here did not collapse stage 2 and stage 3 and the judge understood what he was doing and that the judge made a determination that there was no Batson violation, the determination that you would make at the end of the day. All right? In exchange for my assuming your view on that, I need you to assume my view on something else, which is that the defense counsel understood that that's the stage that they were in; in other words, that now they were talking about the ultimate Batson violation, and what she was objecting to was the finding of the ultimate Batson violation. So let's say that they were both on the same page. We understand that we're contesting the ultimate Batson violation. So if you assume those two things the court says there was no violation, and the defense counsel says I object to that, I think there was a Batson violation, and he said I got that, I understand that, it's preserved, and she says I want to make an argument about this, and he says it's preserved, it's preserved -- how could it not be preserved?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I would -- I agree with the state that there's a distinction between observing -- preserving a claim and preserving particular arguments. And simply saying I disagree with the ultimate finding is not sufficient to preserve every possible pretext argument under the sun. And so I think there needed to be some form of even gesturing at juror comparisons. There was nothing in the record at all until after trial to suggest that Petitioner's counsel thought that there was a problem with white jurors not being struck who shared characteristics with the black jurors who were struck.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So you think in the -- in the face of the court saying, again and again, it's in the record, I got it, it's preserved, she has to make clear what the particulars of her view is, why exactly there is a Batson violation?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: In order to -- to preserve those particular pretext arguments for appeal, yes, under Mississippi law.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch?

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: In fact, a lot of courts have issue preservation rules with respect to arguments, not just claims or issues.

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And we've never held those impermissible before?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: No, Your Honor, and in fact, this Court has repeatedly reaffirmed both -- in the context of Batson claims in state courts, in Batson and in Ford versus Georgia, the Court has reaffirmed that procedural rules are permissible. And then in Singleton versus Wulff, this Court affirmed that courts of appeals in the federal system have discretion over when to accept unpreserved arguments on appeal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh? Justice Barrett?

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Just one question, Ms. Ferguson. Is it unusual for the United States to appear as an amicus when we have a constitutional question before us that affects the United States in litigation?

turns.name: MS. FERGUSON
turns.text: No, Your Honor. It's quite common for us to appear.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr. Perkovich.

turns.name: MR. PERKOVICH
turns.text: Just a few quick points. First I wanted to address the -- the issue with may not in the Woodward case that constrained on direct review the inquiry. And that's simply the point. At that gateway issue, there was an understanding that they clearly may and this Court has done so routinely. So that's our fundamental point on that. With respect to the procedural sort of rules that Ford versus Georgia endorse as a basic principle, we have no qualm with that. That is a timely objection rule. Of course that's not applicable here. And -- and with respect to a waiver rule and forfeiture, however it's framed, the problem is that it's not implementing Batson in step 3. It's obstructing Batson in step 3. And that's the work that's occurring in this case and the problem with that rule. With respect to the conflation of race-neutral findings and step 3, there is a very big step between determining something is race-neutral and determining it is credible. And that's where the work of the court at a minimum, the trial judge, is required, whatever is being pressed by the opponent of the strike for the reasons I've pointed to and are spoken to forcefully in Powers with regard to the other stakeholders and the broader stakes for our society, with regard to the Equal Protection Clause in this context. With regard to remedy, Batson, of course, ushered in a prohibition of the discriminatory use of strikes in a way that improved upon Swain and provided a specific framework. And so when that framework breaks down, as it did here, that's a structural error. And so this has to return to its prior posture, much the way the last time this Court found a (d)(2) violation in Miller-El, which is to the district court and for a judgment entered for the Petitioner.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
